sections.heading: For the purposes of this Chapter—
sections.title: Definitions for registration authorities and payment service providers
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in the registration regime. It specifies that 'registering authority' refers to the FCA for certain persons and the Commissioners for others, and defines 'telecommunication, digital and IT payment service provider' as an entity providing payment services through digital devices with specific conditions.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines critical terms for registration authorities and payment service providers, which are essential for understanding who must register and what constitutes a payment service provider under the DTIA framework.

Registering authority means the Commissioners for relevant persons they are required to register under regulation 54(2) or decide to register under regulation 55(3)
Telecommunication, digital and IT payment service provider means an undertaking providing payment services where consent is given via telecommunication, digital or IT device

sections.source_quote: 53. sections.title: Fit and proper test for financial institutions
sections.summary: The registering authority must refuse registration for money service businesses or trust/company service providers if any applicant, officer, manager, or beneficial owner is not a fit and proper person. This includes individuals convicted under Schedule 3 and those failing to meet risk-based criteria like compliance history, money laundering risks, and professional conduct. The FCA may consider applicant opinions and consult supervisory authorities when determining fit and proper status.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes the fit and proper test for financial institutions, requiring thorough due diligence on applicants and their associates to prevent money laundering and terrorist financing. It impacts DTIAs by mandating rigorous risk assessments for third-party providers and cross-border data transfers involving financial institutions.

sections.source_quote: The registering authority must refuse to register an applicant for registration in a register maintained under regulation 54 as a money service business or as a trust or company service provider, if it is satisfied that— (a) the applicant; (b) an officer or manager of the applicant; (c) a beneficial owner of the applicant; or (d) where the applicant is a money service business— (i) any agent used by the applicant for the purposes of its business; or (ii) any officer, manager or beneficial owner of the agent, is not a fit and proper person to carry on that business.

sections.title: Registration suspension and cancellation procedures
sections.summary: The registering authority may suspend or cancel registration for financial institutions if they fail the fit and proper test, breach compliance requirements, or fail to provide necessary information. This includes specific provisions for cryptoasset businesses and payment service providers. The authority must provide notice and appeal rights before taking action.
sections.dtia_summary: This section outlines procedures for suspending or canceling registrations when institutions fail the fit and proper test or other compliance requirements, directly impacting DTIAs by requiring thorough due diligence on third-party providers and cross-border data transfers involving financial institutions.

sections.title: Definition of registered cryptoasset business
sections.summary: This section defines a 'registered cryptoasset business' as a business registered under the Money Laundering Regulations 2017, specifically within the meaning provided by regulation 59(8). The definition is used to determine which entities are subject to the regulatory framework for cryptoasset businesses. This definition applies to financial institutions and entities involved in cryptoasset transactions.
sections.dtia_summary: This definition is relevant for DTIAs as it specifies which entities must comply with cryptoasset-related data transfer obligations under the regulations.

sections.title: Definition of transfer of funds supervisory authority for payment service providers
sections.summary: This section defines the 'transfer of funds supervisory authority' for payment service providers as the supervisory authority specified by regulation 62. The definition applies to payment service providers, including authorized persons, payment institutions, electronic money institutions, and small payment institutions that are not excluded from certain registers. This definition is critical for determining regulatory oversight responsibilities.
sections.dtia_summary: This definition is relevant for DTIAs as it clarifies which regulatory authority oversees payment service providers, impacting data transfer compliance.

sections.title: Transfer of funds supervisory authority for payment service providers
sections.summary: This section designates the Financial Conduct Authority (FCA) as the transfer of funds supervisory authority for payment service providers not covered by paragraph (1). It mandates that the supervisory authority must monitor payment service providers, ensure compliance with funds transfer regulations, encourage reporting of breaches, and cooperate with other authorities to counter money laundering and terrorist financing. The authority applies to payment service providers with head offices in the UK or abroad.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes the FCA's role in supervising payment service providers across borders, requiring them to report breaches of funds transfer regulations and cooperate internationally to counter money laundering and terrorist financing. It impacts cross-border data transfers by mandating oversight of payment service providers with international head offices.

sections.title: Cryptoasset intermediary risk assessment for money laundering
sections.summary: An intermediary cryptoasset business must assess the risk of money laundering, terrorist financing, and proliferation financing related to inter-cryptoasset transfers. This assessment must consider the purpose and nature of the business relationship with customers and the value of transfers. The business must also report repeated failures by cryptoasset businesses to provide required information.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires cryptoasset intermediaries to evaluate transfer risks and report information failures, impacting cross-border cryptoasset data flows and compliance with anti-money laundering obligations.

requirements.text: An intermediary cryptoasset business must assess the level of risk of money laundering, terrorist financing and proliferation financing arising from inter-cryptoasset business transfers.
An intermediary cryptoasset business must take account of the purpose and nature of the business relationship with its customer cryptoasset business and the inter-cryptoasset business transfer.
An intermediary cryptoasset business must take account of the value of the inter-cryptoasset business transfer and any cryptoasset transfer which appears to be linked.
An intermediary cryptoasset business must report repeated failure by a cryptoasset business to provide information required by regulation 64C.
An intermediary cryptoasset business must report any steps taken in response to such failures.

sections.source_quote: An intermediary cryptoasset business must— (a) ensure that all the information that is provided in relation to an inter-cryptoasset business transfer pursuant to regulation 64C, including any that is requested to be provided before the transfer is made under regulation 64E(2)(a), also accompanies the onward transfer

sections.title: Cross-border data sharing with foreign authorities for AML
sections.summary: Supervisory authorities may request foreign authorities to assist in obtaining information or documents when UK-based entities are involved in cross-border money laundering investigations. The authority may require foreign authorities to contribute to costs and comply with conditions to share information. Foreign authorities must not share information for purposes other than the original request or related legal proceedings.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines conditions for sharing financial data with foreign authorities in cross-border AML investigations, affecting data transfer between jurisdictions and compliance with international financial intelligence requirements.

requirements.text: A supervisory authority may exercise the power conferred by regulation 66 as if it also referred to information and documents reasonably required by the supervisory authority to meet a request from a foreign authority.
The request must be made by a foreign authority in connection with functions provided for in the fourth money laundering directive, funds transfer regulation, or equivalent law in a third country.

The supervisory authority may take into account whether corresponding assistance would be given in the foreign territory, whether the case involves jurisdictional issues, and the seriousness of the case.
The foreign authority must make a contribution towards the cost of the request and comply with conditions set by the supervisory authority.

sections.source_quote: A supervisory authority may exercise the power conferred by regulation 66, and for these purposes, regulation 66 has effect as if it also referred to information and documents reasonably required by the supervisory authority to meet such a request.

sections.title: Warrant execution and safeguards for financial investigations
sections.summary: This section outlines the conditions under which warrants can be executed for financial investigations. It specifies that warrants may be exercised by any executing officer, may authorize persons to accompany executing officers, and may be issued subject to conditions. The powers can be exercised by persons authorized by the warrant but only under the supervision of an executing officer. These warrants are subject to specific legal safeguards under the Police and Criminal Evidence Acts in England and Wales and Northern Ireland.
sections.dtia_summary: Warrants for financial investigations require specific execution procedures and supervision to ensure lawful data handling during investigations, impacting how financial data is collected and processed across borders.

sections.summary: This section mandates that cryptoasset exchange providers and custodian wallet providers must provide specific information to the FCA regarding compliance with regulatory requirements, costs of supervision, and other matters necessary for the FCA's supervisory functions. The information must be provided at specified times and in forms directed by the FCA, verified in a manner specified by the FCA.

sections.summary: This section requires the FCA to publish information about notices given to cryptoasset businesses regarding directions, including the status of appeals to the Upper Tribunal. The FCA must publish such information without delay in the same manner as the original information, and must publish information about the outcome of appeals if the cryptoasset business refers the matter to the Upper Tribunal.

sections.title: Supervisory authority powers for injunctions
sections.summary: This section allows a designated supervisory authority to request court intervention for specific contraventions. The court may issue restraining orders if there is a reasonable likelihood of future contraventions or repeated contraventions. It also permits orders requiring corrective actions for existing contraventions and asset freezing orders for potential contraventions. These powers apply to persons who have contravened financial regulations and are subject to the supervisory authority's oversight. The Commissioners must provide specific notice to individuals when imposing penalties, publishing statements, or imposing prohibitions under regulations 76, 77, or 78. The notice must detail the decision, the amount or terms of the penalty or prohibition, the reasons for the decision, the right to review under regulation 94, and the right to appeal under regulation 99. This applies to individuals subject to these regulatory actions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes judicial mechanisms for enforcing compliance with financial regulations through injunctions, which could impact data transfer if the data subject to transfer is involved in contraventions requiring court intervention. This section mandates specific notice requirements for penalty or prohibition decisions, which impacts DTIAs by requiring clear communication of decisions and rights to review or appeal.

sections.title: Exemption from liability for offences under certain conditions
sections.summary: This section states that a person is not guilty of an offence under regulation 79 if they took all reasonable steps and exercised all due diligence to avoid committing the offence. It also specifies that a person convicted under this regulation is not subject to sanctions under Chapter 2 of the Part. The provision applies to individuals who have taken necessary precautions to prevent the offence. This section specifies that certain legal procedures for charging offences against corporations apply to designated bodies. It mandates that the procedures outlined in regulations 76, 77, and 78 follow the same rules as they do for corporations under specific acts and schedules. These provisions ensure that designated bodies are treated consistently with corporate entities in legal proceedings.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides an exemption from criminal liability for offences related to money laundering if due diligence is exercised, which may impact DTIA by reducing the risk of penalties for non-compliance with transfer requirements.

sections.title: Offence liability for corporate officers in money laundering
sections.summary: This section states that if a body corporate commits an offence under this Part with the consent or connivance of an officer, or due to the officer's neglect, the officer is jointly liable with the corporation. Similarly, for partnerships and unincorporated associations, the officer is liable if the offence was committed with their consent or connivance or due to their neglect. The section applies to officers managing the entity's affairs.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes that officers of corporations, partnerships, or unincorporated associations can be held jointly liable for money laundering offences committed by the entity, which impacts DTIA assessments by requiring consideration of officer liability in cross-border financial transactions.

requirements.text: If an offence under this Part is committed with the consent or connivance of an officer, the officer is jointly liable with the body corporate.
If an offence under this Part is attributable to any neglect on the part of an officer, the officer is jointly liable with the body corporate.
For partnerships, if an offence is committed with the consent or connivance of an officer, the officer is jointly liable with the partnership.
For partnerships, if an offence is attributable to any neglect on the part of an officer, the officer is jointly liable with the partnership.
For unincorporated associations (other than partnerships), if an offence is committed with the consent or connivance of an officer, the officer is jointly liable with the association.
For unincorporated associations (other than partnerships), if an offence is attributable to any neglect on the part of an officer, the officer is jointly liable with the association.

sections.source_quote: If an offence under this Part committed by a body corporate is shown—(a) to have been committed with the consent or the connivance of an officer of the body corporate; or (b) to be attributable to any neglect on the part of an officer, the officer (as well as the body corporate) is guilty of the offence and is liable to be proceeded against and punished accordingly.

sections.heading: CHAPTER 2
sections.title: Review procedures for Commissioners' decisions
sections.summary: This section outlines the process for reviewing decisions made by the Commissioners, including the offer of review, acceptance of the review within 30 days, extensions of time, and the nature of the review. The review must consider steps taken before the review and may conclude that the decision is upheld, varied, or cancelled. The Commissioners must notify the person of the review conclusions within 45 days or as agreed.
sections.dtia_summary: This section details the review procedures for Commissioners' decisions, which is relevant for DTIA assessments as it affects how decisions on cross-border financial transactions are reviewed and appealed, impacting the assessment of regulatory compliance.

The review must take account of any representations made by the person at a stage giving the Commissioners a reasonable opportunity to consider them.
The review may conclude that the decision is upheld, varied, or cancelled.
The Commissioners must give notice of the review conclusions within 45 days or as agreed.

sections.source_quote: The Commissioners must review a decision if—(a) they have offered a review of the decision under this Chapter, and (b) the person concerned notifies the Commissioners that they accept the offer within 30 days from the date of the notice of the offer of a review.

sections.title: Charges and penalties as debts recoverable from financial entities
sections.summary: The FCA and Commissioners may impose charges on applicants for regulatory approvals, registered entities, supervised persons, and payment service providers. These charges must not exceed amounts reasonably needed to cover expenses incurred in carrying out their functions. Charges can apply to each business location where activities are conducted. The FCA must pay penalties to the Treasury annually, with Treasury directions on payment timing and information reporting.
sections.dtia_summary: Charges imposed by regulators on financial entities for supervision must be accounted for in DTIA as they represent financial obligations that could impact costs and compliance with tax requirements.

sections.title: Review of regulatory provisions
sections.summary: The Treasury must periodically review regulatory provisions and publish reports on the conclusions of reviews. Reviews must consider implementation in other member states of the emission allowance auctioning regulation, fourth money laundering directive, and funds transfer regulation.

sections.heading: Schedule 7 makes amendments relating to these Regulations.
sections.title: Revocation and saving provisions
sections.summary: The old money laundering regulations and transfer of funds regulations are revoked. However, they continue to have effect for conduct that began before the regulations came into force. Penalties under the new regulations do not apply in such circumstances. The 'old money laundering regulations' include specific regulations from 2007 to 2015, and the 'old transfer of funds regulations' refer to the 2007 regulations.

The change ensures that deferred prosecution arrangements for money laundering offenses align with the latest regulatory framework.
sections.dtia_summary: This section updates the Crime and Courts Act to incorporate the 2017 money laundering regulations, ensuring that financial institutions comply with international standards for cross-border financial data transfers.

requirements.text: In paragraph 27 of Schedule 17 to the Crime and Courts Act 2013, substitute 'regulation 86 of the Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017' for 'regulation 45 of the Money Laundering Regulations 2:07 (S.I. 2007/2157)'

sections.source_quote: In Schedule 17 (offences in relation to which a deferred prosecution arrangement may be entered into) to the Crime and Courts Act 2013 M224, in paragraph 27, for "regulation 45 of the Money Laundering Regulations 2007 (S.I. 2007/2157)" substitute "regulation 86 of the Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017".

sections.heading: In section 1 (general customs functions of the Secretary of State) of the Borders, Citizenship
sections.title: Secure transmission of information to foreign FIUs
sections.summary: This section mandates that the NCA must take appropriate steps to ensure that information provided to foreign FIUs in response to external requests is transmitted securely. This establishes a security requirement for cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: The NCA must take such steps as it considers appropriate to ensure that information provided to foreign FIUs in response to external requests is transmitted securely

sections.source_quote: 8. Where the NCA provides relevant information in response to an external request in accordance with this Schedule, the NCA shall take such steps as it considers appropriate to ensure that such information is transmitted securely.

sections.heading: In Schedule 17 (offences in relation to which a deferred prosecution arrangement may be
sections.title: NCA's authority to restrict information use in external requests
sections.summary: The National Crime Agency (NCA) can impose restrictions and conditions on how relevant information provided in response to external requests is used, as determined by the NCA. This authority applies to information shared with the NCA during investigations or requests for data. The NCA may limit the use of such information to prevent misuse or protect national interests.
sections.dtia_summary: This section allows the NCA to restrict how information from foreign financial intelligence units (FIUs) is used, which impacts cross-border data transfers by requiring specific safeguards for information sharing.

requirements.text: The NCA may impose restrictions and conditions on the use of relevant information provided in response to an external request

sections.source_quote: 9. The NCA may impose such restrictions and conditions on the use of relevant information provided in response to an external request as the NCA considers appropriate.

sections.heading: Regulation 48 (bearer shares) of the Open-Ended Investment Companies Regulations 2001
sections.title: Amend Open-Ended Investment Companies Regulations 2001 for bearer shares
sections.summary: This section amends Regulation 48 of the Open-Ended Investment Companies Regulations 2001 by renumbering the existing text as paragraph (1) and inserting new paragraphs (2) and (3). Paragraph (2) prohibits open-ended investment companies authorized on or after the relevant date from issuing bearer shares, rendering any provisions in their instruments of incorporation authorizing such shares void. Paragraph (3) provides an exception for companies where an authorization application was made before the relevant date but not determined until after that date.

requirements.text: Renumber existing text as paragraph (1) in Regulation 48 of the Open-Ended Investment Companies Regulations 2001
Insert paragraph (2): Open-ended investment companies authorized on or after the relevant date may not issue bearer shares
Insert paragraph (3): Paragraph (2) does not apply to companies where an authorization application was made before the relevant date but not determined until after the relevant date

sections.source_quote: 18. Regulation 48 (bearer shares) of the Open-Ended Investment Companies Regulations 2001 M234 is amended as follows— (a) the existing text is renumbered as paragraph (1); (b) in that paragraph (1), after "investment company" insert " authorised before the day on which the Money Laundering, Terrorist Financing and Transfer of Funds (Information on the Payer) Regulations 2017 came into force ("the relevant date") "; (c) after paragraph (1) insert— (2) An open-ended investment company authorized on or after the relevant date may not issue any bearer shares under paragraph (1), and any provision in the instrument of incorporation of such an open-ended investment company purporting to authorise it to do so is void. (3) Paragraph (2) does not apply to an open-ended investment company if— (a) an application for an authorisation order was made in relation to that open-ended investment company before the relevant date; and (b) that application was not determined until a date on or after the relevant date.
